main_ingredients: chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage, Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of stir-fried chye sim in Singapore is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small plate or bowl served alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried Chye Sim — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for stir-fried chye sim (F14080045). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~1.8 g/100g based on typical vegetable oil composition (predominantly unsaturated) consistent with HPB data. The HPB Foodo database entry F14080045 reports only the following fields: energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron. All remaining micronutrient and fatty acid fraction fields are not reported in this source entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Non-Starchy Leafy Vegetables — Analogous Estimate Based on Cooked Leafy Brassica Vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried chye sim in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 15 (low) based on analogous cooked non-starchy leafy Brassica vegetables (e.g., cooked Chinese cabbage, GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (15 × 5.18) / 100 ≈ 0.8. The very low carbohydrate content (3.45 g/100g) means glycaemic impact is negligible regardless of GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 471 mg/100g from added soy sauce, oyster sauce, and salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced seasoning, as a 150 g serving delivers approximately 707 mg sodium — nearly 31% of the 2300 mg daily limit — and the dish should be avoided or modified by omitting soy sauce and oyster sauce to bring sodium to a safer level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.45 g/100g), zero sugar, and negligible glycaemic index (~15)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried chye sim is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycaemic load (GL ≈ 0.8 per 150 g serving); however, the high fat content (13 g/100g) from cooking oil contributes to caloric density and should be moderated by requesting less oil.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (13 g/100g) and elevated sodium (471 mg/100g) from cooking oil and seasonings
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While saturated fat is relatively low (~1.8 g/100g) and the vegetable base provides beneficial calcium (125 mg/100g) and iron (1.89 mg/100g), the high sodium content and total fat from added oil are concerns for cardiovascular disease management; patients should request the dish prepared with minimal oil and reduced soy sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory vegetable base with low saturated fat; elevated sodium may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chye sim provides calcium (125 mg/100g) and iron (1.89 mg/100g) beneficial for bone and blood health in rheumatoid arthritis; however, sodium at 471 mg/100g may exacerbate tissue swelling and patients should request reduced seasoning.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried chye sim (炒菜心) is a classic Singaporean Chinese side dish made from chye sim (Chinese flowering cabbage, Brassica rapa subsp. parachinensis), a tender leafy green vegetable with mild, slightly sweet flavour and crisp-tender texture when stir-fried. The dish is prepared by stir-frying chye sim in a wok over high heat with cooking oil, garlic, soy sauce, and oyster sauce until fragrant and just wilted, preserving the vegetable's bright green colour and nutritional integrity. It is a staple accompaniment to rice meals in Singapore hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and home kitchens. Per 100 g, the dish provides 140 kcal, 2.82 g protein, 13.01 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), 3.45 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 0.46 g dietary fibre, 471 mg sodium, 125 mg calcium, and 1.89 mg iron. The high fat content relative to the vegetable base reflects the generous use of cooking oil typical of hawker-style preparation. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~15, GL ~0.8 per serving), but the elevated sodium (471 mg/100g) makes it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management unless prepared with reduced seasoning. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to oyster sauce, and contains soy and shellfish allergens.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14080045) with confidence 0.95; this is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The food identity is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from typical vegetable oil composition as HPB does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this entry.
notes: sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (F14080045); chye sim is a non-starchy leafy vegetable with negligible natural sugars and no added sugar in this preparation. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080045 does not report these values, and no other approved source provides a directly applicable breakdown for this specific stir-fried preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions are not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045 and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact Singaporean hawker preparation; potassium_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; cholesterol_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_c_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_b6_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_b12_mcg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_d_mcg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; vitamin_e_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; phosphorus_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; magnesium_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; zinc_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; selenium_mcg — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045; water_g — not reported in HPB Foodo F14080045. Source consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/), entry F14080045, accessed 2026-04-16. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked non-starchy leafy Brassica vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried chye sim. The dish is not vegan due to oyster sauce; a vegan variant can be prepared by substituting oyster sauce with mushroom sauce.
